one_line_takeaway: A framework-grounded position paper arguing that LLMs are a genuine but brittle double-edged force in red/blue teaming, and that responsible adoption hinges on human oversight, governance, and — above all — realistic operational benchmarks that do not yet exist.
key_finding: LLMs are fluent, versatile, and useful across cybersecurity tasks but remain brittle in high-stakes, context-rich operational environments. Concretely, constrained context retention, hallucinations, reasoning deficiencies, and prompt sensitivity undermine reliability in multi-stage red-team simulations and blue-team detection/response. Current applications are narrow and language-centric, falling short on data-intensive, action-oriented demands like lateral movement, dynamic response, and real-time adaptation. A recurring cross-cutting finding is that the field lacks realistic, real-world benchmarks: CTF challenges and synthetic datasets fail to capture operational complexity, and LLM offensive performance (per cited work) drops sharply beyond the easiest CTF levels due to reasoning/knowledge limits. The authors argue governance, standardization, human-in-the-loop oversight, and real-world evaluation are urgently needed.
core_contribution: A position paper that systematically maps how LLMs intersect with cybersecurity red-team (offensive) and blue-team (defensive) operations by organizing the analysis around two established frameworks: MITRE ATT&CK (for adversary tactics/techniques) and the NIST Cybersecurity Framework (CSF) 2.0 (for defensive core functions). Its own contribution is conceptual, not empirical: (1) two structured reference tables tying framework elements to LLM-automatable tasks (Table 1 highlights 7 of the 14 MITRE ATT&CK tactics; Table 2 organizes blue-team work into the 6 NIST CSF core functions govern/identify/protect/detect/respond/recover); (2) a taxonomy of emergent LLM strengths (5 red-team and 5 blue-team capability areas); (3) a taxonomy of technical challenges (context/short-term-memory limits, hallucinations, reasoning limits, prompt/tuning sensitivity, evaluation & integration gaps); (4) a taxonomy of operational risks (dual-use, over-reliance, under-reliance, emerging-tech risk, privacy, agentic-LLM risk); and (5) seven sets of strategic recommendations for responsible adoption. Note: the empirical claims about specific LLM pentesting/exploitation abilities (SQL injection, CVE exploitation, CTF performance) are drawn from cited prior work, not experiments the authors ran.
threat_model: The paper frames a broad dual-use threat landscape rather than a single formal attacker model. Central concern: LLMs lower the technical and resource barrier to sophisticated offensive cyber operations, letting low-skilled actors (script-kiddies) operate with capability once reserved for advanced persistent threats, and blurring the amateur/state-sponsored distinction. Adversaries repurpose red-team tooling (Cobalt Strike, Metasploit, PowerSploit) and openly accessible LLMs to automate reconnaissance, craft phishing, and generate evasive/exploit code. The paper cites CrowdStrike's 2024 Global Threat Report noting the group Scattered Spider likely used an LLM to generate PowerShell scripts in a 2023 attack. Secondary threat surfaces include privacy leakage via proprietary-model API use, erosion of human oversight, and enlarged attack surfaces of agentic LLM systems (it references CVE-2025-31491 as an agentic-platform vulnerability from which credentials could be leaked).
limitations: As a position paper it presents no original experiments, benchmarks, or quantitative evaluation to substantiate its capability/limitation claims — it explicitly calls for the very real-world benchmarks it cannot provide. Evidence is anecdotal or borrowed from cited studies and industry reports. The two framework tables are illustrative and simplified (7 of 14 ATT&CK tactics; example techniques only) rather than exhaustive. Recommendations are high-level strategic principles without implementation detail, cost analysis, or validation of effectiveness. Some headline numbers rely on non-peer-reviewed sources (news articles, vendor threat reports).

quantitative_results: No original quantitative results — position paper with no experiments of its own
Cited context (not authors' measurements): estimated cybercrime cost of $9.5 trillion in 2024, forecast exceeding $10.5 trillion in 2025 [ref 24]
Cited context: Ticketmaster breach impacting 560 million users
Cited context: Amazon reported facing nearly 1 billion cyber threats per day (WSJ, Nov 2024) [ref 29]
Structural: highlights 7 of the 14 MITRE ATT&CK tactics (Table 1); NIST CSF organized into 6 core functions (Table 2); 5 red-team and 5 blue-team LLM capability areas enumerated
